From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Accent of English spoken in Bangladesh

This article is about the accent of the English language. For English people of Bangladeshi descent, see [British Bangladeshi](/wiki/British_Bangladeshi "British Bangladeshi"). For the mixed use of Bengali and English languages, see [Banglish](/wiki/Banglish "Banglish").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w.svg) | This article **is missing information** about pronunciation. Please expand the article to include this information. Further details may exist on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Bangladeshi_English "Talk:Bangladeshi English"). *(December 2024)* |

| Bangladeshi English | |
| --- | --- |
| Native to | [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") |
| [Language family](/wiki/Language_family "Language family") | [Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages")  * [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages")   + [West Germanic](/wiki/West_Germanic_languages "West Germanic languages")     - [North Sea Germanic](/wiki/North_Sea_Germanic "North Sea Germanic")       * [Anglo-Frisian](/wiki/Anglo-Frisian_languages "Anglo-Frisian languages")         + [English](/wiki/English_language "English language")           - [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English")             * [South Asian English](/wiki/South_Asian_English "South Asian English")               + **Bangladeshi English** |
| Early forms | [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language")  * [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic_language "Proto-Germanic language")   + [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English")     - [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English")       * [Early Modern English](/wiki/Early_Modern_English "Early Modern English") |
| [Writing system](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") | [Latin](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") ([English alphabet](/wiki/English_alphabet "English alphabet")) [Unified English Braille](/wiki/Unified_English_Braille "Unified English Braille") |
| Language codes | |
| [ISO 639-3](/wiki/ISO_639-3 "ISO 639-3") | – |
| [IETF](/wiki/IETF_language_tag "IETF language tag") | `en-BD` |

**Bangladeshi English** is a nativized variety of the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") that has evolved since its introduction in Bangladesh primarily as a medium of administration, education, and commerce during [British colonial rule](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") in the eighteenth century.[[1]](#cite_note-f865-1)

After [independence in 1971](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War"), amid nationalist efforts to decolonize institutions, Bengali was established as the sole state language. But English still retained a significant role in higher education, government documents, judicial proceedings, and mass media, alongside remaining a compulsory subject in primary and secondary education.[[1]](#cite_note-f865-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Linguistically, Bangladeshi English is distinguished by phonological features deeply influenced by the Bengali substrate. These include the [monophthongization](/wiki/Monophthongization "Monophthongization") of [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"), the neutralization of aspiration contrasts, and the absence of certain English consonant clusters and vowel lengths in [Bengali phonology](/wiki/Bengali_phonology "Bengali phonology"). Its lexical innovations include the addition of Bengali loanwords to express local flora, fauna, and cultural concepts; calques and simplified syntactic structures, adapted for non-native speakers using it as a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") in multinational professional environments, are also observed.[[3]](#cite_note-w649-3)

Classified as an emerging postcolonial variety within the framework of [World Englishes](/wiki/World_Englishes "World Englishes"), this language exemplifies the nativization processes observed in other [South Asian Englishes](/wiki/South_Asian_English "South Asian English"). Some critics opine that deficient pronunciation has hindered intelligibility.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[3]](#cite_note-w649-3)

## Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bangladeshi_English&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Use")]

[Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") is the sole [official](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") and [national language](/wiki/National_language "National language") of Bangladesh. However, English is often used secondarily in the higher tier of the judiciary in the country. Laws were written in English during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").

Since the introduction of [Bangla Bhasha Procholon Ain](/wiki/Bangla_Bhasha_Procholon_Ain,_1987 "Bangla Bhasha Procholon Ain, 1987"), all the laws by parliament and all Ordinances promulgated by the President are being enacted in Bengali[[5]](#cite_note-5) in [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh").

There are ten English language newspapers in Bangladesh. English medium schools are also operated in English. Mainly, the people of Bangladeshi descent residing in the UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand and the US and students of English medium schools in Bangladesh use Benglish (though the teaching of Standard English is also attempted).

However, upon public demand in 2012, the High Court of Bangladesh banned the use of Benglish, described as a slang mixture of Bengali and English, in radio and television programs "to protect local tongue".[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Benglish is a term that has been used in academic papers to describe a mixture of Bangla and English. For example, Benglish verbs are described as a particular type of complex predicate that consists of an English word and a Bengali verb, such as *æksiḍenṭ kôra* 'to have an accident', *in kôra* 'to get/come/put in' or *kônfyus kôra* 'to confuse'.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bangladeshi_English&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") adopted English as the official language of the empire in 1835. Replacement of the [Persian language](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") with English was followed by a surge in English language learning among [Bengali babus](/wiki/Babu_(title) "Babu (title)"). English remained an official language of the region until 1956 when the first constitution of Pakistan was adopted stating Bengali and Urdu as the official languages of the state following the [Bengali language movement](/wiki/Bengali_language_movement "Bengali language movement") from 1947 to 1952.

After independence in 1971, Bengali became the sole official language of Bangladesh, and all English-medium universities, schools and colleges were converted to Bengali instruction in order to spread mass education.

## Phonological features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bangladeshi_English&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Phonological features")]

The influence of the Bengali substrate is evident in Bangladeshi English vowel pronunciation, which neutralizes the English tense-lax distinction. For example, /iː/ and /ɪ/ merge into /i/, and /uː/ and /ʊ/ merge into /u/.[[10]](#cite_note-e885m-10) Back and central vowels such as /ɑː/, /ʌ/, and /ɜː/ converge towards the low central /a/, and the schwa /ə/ is realized as /æ/. The trap-bath split, characteristic of Received Pronunciation, is absent.[[10]](#cite_note-e885m-10) The TRAP, BATH, and often STRUT lexical sets merge into the /a/ phoneme.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The monophthongization of English complex diphthongs occurs, similar to Bengali's simpler gliding types, resulting in /eɪ/ reducing to /e/, /əʊ/ to /o/, and /eə/ to /e/.[[10]](#cite_note-e885m-10)

Some varieties maintain distinctions lost in modern RP, such as [that between pre-rhotic /oʊ/ and /ɔː/](/wiki/English-language_vowel_changes_before_historic_/r/#Horse–hoarse_merger "English-language vowel changes before historic /r/").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Deviations in the consonant inventory include the substitution of fricatives that are absent in Bengali. /θ/ and /ð/ are typically pronounced as the aspirated dentals /tʰ/ and /dʰ/, respectively, and /v/ emerges as a bilabial /b/ or /β/. The voiced alveolar /z/ phoneme is often affricated to /dʒ/, resulting in the pronunciation of words like "zoo" as /dʒuː/.[[14]](#cite_note-h74b6-14) Syllable-final /r/ is not elided, being pronounced as a trill or flap, and word-initial /r/ shows a retroflex tendency, close to the Bengali /ɽ/, thus producing an approximant sound.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-h74b6-14)

At the suprasegmental level, rhythm adopts Bengali's syllable-timing; the durations of stressed and unstressed syllables become comparable, and these deviate from stress-timed norms, as measured in phonetic analyses by metrics such as ΔC and %V. In terms of intonation, a rising contour is used for yes-no questions and a falling contour for statements, and focus on any part is marked by high-low contours.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bangladeshi_English&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Literature")]

Main article: [Bangladeshi English literature](/wiki/Bangladeshi_English_literature "Bangladeshi English literature")

Bangladeshi English literature (BEL) refers to the body of literary work written in the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language") in [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") and the [Bangladeshi diaspora](/wiki/Bangladeshi_diaspora "Bangladeshi diaspora"). In academia, it is also now referred to as Bangladeshi Writing in English (BWE).[[17]](#cite_note-17) Early prominent Bengali writers in English include [Ram Mohan Roy](/wiki/Raja_Ram_Mohan_Roy "Raja Ram Mohan Roy"), [Bankim Chandra Chatterjee](/wiki/Bankim_Chandra_Chatterjee "Bankim Chandra Chatterjee"), [Begum Rokeya](/wiki/Begum_Rokeya "Begum Rokeya"), and [Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore"). In 1905, Begum Rokeya (1880–1932) wrote *[Sultana's Dream](/wiki/Sultana%27s_Dream "Sultana's Dream")*, one of the earliest examples of [feminist science fiction](/wiki/Feminist_science_fiction "Feminist science fiction").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Modern writers of the Bangladeshi diaspora include [Tahmima Anam](/wiki/Tahmima_Anam "Tahmima Anam"), [Neamat Imam](/wiki/Neamat_Imam "Neamat Imam"), [Monica Ali](/wiki/Monica_Ali "Monica Ali"), and [Zia Haider Rahman](/wiki/Zia_Haider_Rahman "Zia Haider Rahman").

## Numbering system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bangladeshi_English&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Numbering system")]

The [South Asian numbering system](/wiki/South_Asian_numbering_system "South Asian numbering system") is preferred for digit grouping. When written in words, or when spoken,
numbers less than 100,000/100 000 are expressed just as they are in [Standard English](/wiki/Standard_English "Standard English"). Numbers including and beyond 100,000 /
100 000 are expressed in a subset of the [South Asian numbering system](/wiki/South_Asian_numbering_system "South Asian numbering system").

Thus, the following scale is used:

| In digits | In words ([long and short scales](/wiki/Long_and_short_scales "Long and short scales")) | In words (South Asian system) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 10 | ten | |
| 100 | one hundred | |
| 1,000 | one thousand | |
| 10,000 | ten thousand | |
| 100,000 | one hundred thousand | one [lakh](/wiki/Lakh "Lakh") |
| 1,000,000 | one million | ten lakh |
| 10,000,000 | ten million | one [crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore") |

## Grammar and Words

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bangladeshi_English&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Grammar and Words")]

The words used to speak in Bangladesh

| American English | British English | Bangladeshi English |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Counter-clockwise | Anti-clockwise | Anti-clockwise |
| Licence plate | Number plate | Licence plate/Number plate |
| Soccer | Football | Football |
| Sweet | Candy | Sweet/Candy (they mean different things in Bangladesh) |
| French fries | Chips | French fries |
| Chips | Crisps | Chips |
| Cookie | Biscuit | Biscuit |
| Store | Shop/retailer (formal)/store (if large) | Store |
| Pharmacy/drugstore | Chemist/pharmacist/pharmacy | Pharmacy |
| Trash Can | Dustbin | Dustbin |
| Movie | Film | Movie/Film |
| Movie Theater | Cinema | Theatre/Cinema Hall |
| Fall | Autumn | Autumn |
| Teller | Cashier | Cashier |
| Vacation | Holiday | Holiday/Vacation |
| (sports) Uniform | Kit | Uniform |
| Sports Field | Pitch/field | Pitch |
| Bathroom | toilet/loo/smallest room/cloakroom | Bathroom/Toilet |
| Mom, Mommy | Mum, Mummy | Mom, Mommy [*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Bangladeshi_English#Dubious "Talk:Bangladeshi English")*] |
| Buddy | Mate/pal/chum/friend | Friend (formal and/or semiformal)/Mate (informal)[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Bangladeshi_English#Dubious "Talk:Bangladeshi English")*] |
| (telephone) Booth | (telephone) kiosk/box | (telephone) booth/Box |
| Police Officer | Policeman | Police Officer |
| Mail | Post | Mail |
| Zip Code | Postcode | Post Code |
| Mailman | Postman | Postman |
| Lawyer | Lawyer (/Solicitor/Barrister - depending on context) | Lawyer/Barrister |
| Cell Phone | Mobile Phone | Mobile Phone |
| Crosswalk | Zebra Crossing | Zebra Crossing |
| Garbage | Rubbish | Garbage (highly used)/Rubbish |
| Railroad | Railway | Railway |
| Overpass | Flyover | Flyover |
| Package | Parcel | Parcel |
| Resume | Curriculum Vitae (CV) | Resume/CV |
| Restroom | Public Toilet | Public Toilet |
| Shopping Cart | Trolley | Trolley |
| Apartment | Flat | Apartment/Flat |
| Mad | Angry | Angry |
| Elevator | Lift | Lift |
| Gas/Gasoline | Petrol | Petrol |
| Cab | Taxi | Taxi Cab |
| Flashlight | Torch | Torch |
| Airplane | Aeroplane | Airplane/Aeroplane |
| Sneakers | Trainers | Trainers |
| Purse | Handbag | Purse/Handbag |
| Diaper | Nappy | Diaper/Nappy |
| Parking lot | Car Park | Parking |
| First floor | Ground floor | Ground floor/First Floor (less common, used only if specifically mentioned) |
| Yard | Garden | Garden |
| Schedule | Timetable | Schedule (More official and common)/Timetable (less common) |
| Pants | Trousers | Pants/Trousers (depending on fabrics) |
| Line | Queue | Line |
| Basement | Basement/cellar | Underground |
| Subway | Underground | Subway |
| Highway | Motorway | Highway |
| Truck | Lorry | Truck/Lorry |
| Scotch tape | Sellotape | Scotch tape |
| Zero | Nil | Zero |
| Eggplant | Aubergine | Brinjal |
| Pitcher | Jug | Jug |
| Wrench | Spanner | Wrench |
| Dessert | Pudding | Pudding (specific item with same name) / Dessert (various kinds of sweet dishes or confections) |
| Eraser | Rubber | Rubber/Eraser |
| Faucet | Tap | Tap |
| Marked Crosswalk | Zebra Crossing | Zebra Crossing |
| Elementary School | Primary School | Primary School |
| Autopsy | Post-mortem | Post-mortem/Autopsy |
| Push-up | Press-up | Push-up |
| Couch | Sofa | Sofa |
| Corn | Maize | Corn |
| Curly Braces | Curly Brackets | Curly Braces |
| Apron | Pinny/Pinafore | Apron |
